text: The surgeon distends the bladder with 180 cc of normal saline, followed by making a vertical cystotomy incision to gain access into the bladder.

Upon entering the bladder, the surgeon lengthens the incision to visualize the prostate, urethra, and bilateral ureteral orifices. Four stay sutures are placed using 2-0 Vicryl on a CT-1 needle, securing the bladder to the abdominal sidewall in superior and inferior positions to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon tensions the four stay sutures with an additional weck to open the cystotomy incision, providing clear visualization of the prostatic adenoma. A 2-0 Vicryl suture on a CT-1 needle with a wet clip is placed through the prostatic adenoma at the inferior position, avoiding the ureteral orifices, and used as a retraction suture with the lateral robotic arm.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to apply tension and makes an incision in the prostatic adenoma at the inferior position, carefully avoiding the entrance of the ureteral orifices into the bladder. This incision creates a plane inferior to the prostatic adenoma, facilitating initial dissection to identify its size and location.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to dissect the fibromuscular aspects of the prostate on the right side, carefully maintaining a medial approach to avoid lateral drift and potential bleeding outside the capsule. The circumferential dissection continues around the prostatic adenoma, progressing to the superior portion while avoiding capsular bleeding, particularly near the dorsal venous complex (DVC) on the superior aspect.

The surgeon dissects the prostatic adenoma away from the capsule, using a vicryl stay suture as a retraction suture through the robotic port. Tension is applied in various directions to pull the adenoma into the bladder. The apex of the prostatic adenoma and the catheter are identified, and the remaining attachments are freed. The prostatic adenoma is then placed into an endocatch bag.

After the removal of the prostatic adenoma, the surgeon advances the urethral mucosa using a 2-0 Vicryl suture with a CT-1 needle. Hemostasis is achieved with electrocautery and hemostatic agents like Floseal, ensuring minimal bleeding in the surgical field.

After achieving hemostasis, the surgeon inserts a 22 French three-way Foley catheter into the bladder. The inflow is capped to monitor hematuria post-procedure. The previously placed suspension sutures are removed, and the bladder closure begins with a running 3-0 V-lock suture on the mucosal layer using a CV-23 needle.

The surgeon closes the muscle layer of the bladder using a two-oh V-lock suture with a GS-XXI needle. After completing the closure, the prostatic adenoma is placed in an endocatch bag. The bladder is then distended to verify its water-tight integrity.
The surgeon places a JP drain in the surgical site before removing the robotic ports.
